examples.title: Union of Two Regular Languages (NFA Construction)
examples.description: Algorithm to construct NFA for L1 ∪ L2 given NFAs for L1 and L2
examples.code:
UNION_NFA(N1, N2):
 // N1 = (Q1, Σ, δ1, s1, F1)
 // N2 = (Q2, Σ, δ2, s2, F2)
 
 Q ← Q1 ∪ Q2 ∪ {s} // Add new start state
 F ← F1 ∪ F2
 δ ← δ1 ∪ δ2
 
 // Add ε-transitions from new start to both original starts
 FOR each a ∈ Σ:
 δ(s, a) ← ∅ // No transitions from s on symbols
 
 δ(s, ε) ← {s1, s2} // Epsilon transitions
 
 RETURN NFA(Q, Σ, δ, s, F)
examples.explanation: Create a new start state s with ε-transitions to both original start states. The NFA accepts a string if either branch accepts it.

examples.title: Product Construction for Intersection
examples.description: Algorithm to construct DFA for L1 ∩ L2 given DFAs for both languages
examples.code:
INTERSECTION_DFA(M1, M2):
 // M1 = (Q1, Σ, δ1, s1, F1)
 // M2 = (Q2, Σ, δ2, s2, F2)
 
 Q ← Q1 × Q2 // All pairs of states
 s ← (s1, s2) // Start state is pair of starts
 F ← F1 × F2 // Accepting if BOTH accept
 
 FOR each state (p, q) ∈ Q:
 FOR each symbol a ∈ Σ:
 δ((p, q), a) ← (δ1(p, a), δ2(q, a))
 
 RETURN DFA(Q, Σ, δ, s, F)
examples.explanation: The product automaton simulates both DFAs simultaneously. A state (p,q) is accepting only if both p and q are accepting, meaning both original DFAs accept the input.

examples.title: Complement of Regular Language
examples.description: Algorithm to construct DFA for complement of a regular language
examples.explanation: Simply flip the accepting status of all states. This works because in a DFA, every string leads to exactly one state, so the complement accepts exactly those strings the original rejected.

examples.title: Homomorphism Application
examples.description: Algorithm to apply homomorphism h to regular language L
examples.code:
APPLY_HOMOMORPHISM(N, h):
 // N = (Q, Σ, δ, s, F) for language L
 // h: Σ → Δ* is a homomorphism
 
 Q' ← Q
 s' ← s
 F' ← F
 δ' ← ∅
 
 FOR each state q ∈ Q:
 FOR each symbol a ∈ Σ:
 // Replace transition on 'a' with transitions on h(a)
 // h(a) could be ε (empty) or string of symbols
 FOR each symbol b ∈ h(a):
 δ'(q, b) ← δ(q, a) // Simulate h(a) step by step
 
 RETURN NFA(Q', Δ, δ', s', F')
examples.explanation: Replace each transition labeled 'a' with transitions that read the string h(a). This transforms the NFA to recognize h(L).

examples.title: Kleene Star Construction
examples.description: Algorithm to construct NFA for L*
examples.explanation: The new accepting start state allows accepting the empty string. The ε-transitions from old accepting states back to the original start allow repeating strings from L any number of times.
